text.title: 30-60-90-day-plan Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for 30-60-90-day-plan. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Every milestone in a 30-60-90 plan must be anchored to a concrete, observable artifact; never use the verbs "understand", "be familiar with", or "learn about" as milestone criteria.
rule.rationale: Vague verbs cannot be verified at the day-30 review; the manager and hire will disagree on whether the milestone is complete. Observable artifacts (PR merged, doc written, customer call completed, deal proposed) have a clear binary: done or not done.

rule.statement: Co-author the plan with the hiring manager and the new hire on Day 1; plans written by HR alone become checkboxes nobody reads after week 2.
rule.rationale: Ownership of the plan predicts adherence. When the new hire participates in setting the milestones, they internalize them as personal commitments rather than corporate requirements.

rule.statement: Days 1-30 are a learning phase; do not assign project ownership or execution pressure in the first 30 days unless the role explicitly requires immediate delivery (e.g., revenue-critical backfill).
rule.rationale: Pushing delivery into the learn phase causes early burnout, poor-quality output, and missed context that creates technical or relationship debt. The day-30 review is the correct gate for confirming readiness to contribute.

rule.statement: Phase 2 ends with ownership of at least one completed project, however small; a completed project is a stronger signal than partial progress on a large one.
rule.rationale: Completing a scoped project demonstrates the full delivery cycle (planning, execution, communication, handoff) at whatever scale is appropriate. A partial large project reveals nothing about completion behavior.

rule.statement: By day 90, the hire must work independently on 80%+ of their responsibilities; anything below this threshold triggers a formal performance conversation, not an extension of the onboarding period.
rule.rationale: Extending the onboarding frame past 90 days without a performance conversation obscures underperformance and delays the manager's decision. The 90-day review is the formal transition from onboarding to standard performance management.

rule.statement: Run the day-30 review at day 25 to leave 5 days of correction opportunity before the formal milestone; never use the review as the first signal of problems.
rule.rationale: If issues surface first at the day-30 review, the correction window is zero. Running at day 25 gives 5 days to course-correct before the formal gate, converting the review from a verdict into a coaching tool.

rule.statement: Engineering 30-60-90 plans must anchor milestones to code artifacts: merged PR, deployed feature, published doc, completed code review — not "learned the codebase".
rule.rationale: Learning the codebase is a means, not a milestone. The day-30 deliverable should be a first commit, however small, that demonstrates the environment is set up and the engineer can work in the team's workflow.

rule.statement: Sales 30-60-90 plans must anchor to pipeline metrics, not activity counts; "made 50 calls" is not a milestone — "built a qualified pipeline of $X" is.
rule.rationale: Activity counts measure effort, not output. A sales representative can make 100 calls to unqualified prospects and have zero qualified pipeline. Pipeline value and deal stage distribution are the leading indicators of ramp performance.
